title: Re-pinning a mortise and tenon joint with a wooden peg
subtitle: Drill and drive a hardwood peg through a loose mortise and tenon joint to restore rigidity without full disassembly
excerpt: How to re-pin a loose mortise and tenon joint in a chair or table using a hardwood peg. Covers drilling through the mortise and tenon, making or buying the peg, driving and trimming the peg flush, and when to use this method versus full re-gluing.
sourceNotes: Newey and Drage, Practical Carpentry and Joinery (1905, public domain). Traditional country furniture peg-joinery technique. City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Restoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hardwood peg (typically oak, ash, or elm) driven through holes in a mortise and tenon joint to lock the joint permanently. In traditional draw-boring, the hole in the tenon is drilled slightly off-centre from the holes in the mortise cheeks, so that driving the peg draws the joint tight. The restoration version uses an aligned hole and relies on PVA glue applied to the peg for holding power, as the joint is typically already assembled. Octagonal pegs produced by riving (splitting) rather than drilling are stiffer under shear than round drilled-rod pegs and are the traditional choice.

content.text: driven through a loose chair or table joint locks it solid without taking the piece apart. Drill a hole through the joint at right angles to the tenon, coat the peg with PVA, and drive it in. The peg bridges the slot walls and the tenon and holds all three together.

Pegging is appropriate when the joint has minor play but the tenon is still largely intact. If the tenon has broken or the mortise walls have cracked, the joint needs re-cutting or a new rail, not pegging. Check by pushing and pulling the joint in every direction before committing to the peg.

Mark the drill position on the outer face of the mortise member, centred on the joint and 15 to 20 mm from the stile edge. Choose a peg diameter of 6 to 10 mm depending on the joint size. Drill through the outer mortise cheek, through the tenon, and out through the inner mortise cheek in one pass (keeping the drill square to the joint face. A drill bit one size smaller than the peg diameter gives a tight press fit.

For a round peg, use a length of dowel in a hard species) oak, ash, or beech. Cut to the joint thickness plus 15 mm. Chamfer the lead end slightly with the chisel so it starts the hole easily. Alternatively, rive a peg from a small piece of straight-grained oak (a riven peg is stiffer than a drilled rod because the grain runs along the peg length.

Apply PVA to the peg surface. Drive the peg into the hole with the mallet, working steadily rather than in one blow. The peg should drive firm without splitting. Allow the PVA to cure) 24 hours. Trim the protruding end flush with a sharp chisel, working across the grain in short strokes. Sand flush with 120-grit.
